--- Thread (6 messages, 2022-05-20) ---
[2022-05-20 04:30] pikachumemes. (Pikachu Memes): Chess is probably the closest demographic, since it is a fully deterministic game
[2022-05-20 04:30] pikachumemes. (Pikachu Memes): Honestly reminds me of FisherRandom, so if there's a space for that you could try advertising there
[2022-05-20 04:31] pikachumemes. (Pikachu Memes): https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fischer_random_chess
[2022-05-20 04:31] pikachumemes. (Pikachu Memes): unfortunately, i dont think this varient is that popular
[2022-05-20 04:32] pikachumemes. (Pikachu Memes): Interesting quote here: "Fischer Random is an interesting format, but it has its drawbacks. In particular, the nontraditional starting positions make it difficult for many amateurs to enjoy the game until more familiar positions are achieved. The same is true for world-class players, as many have confessed to me privately. Finally, it also seems to lack an aesthetic quality found in traditional chess, which makes it less appealing for both players and viewers, even if it does occasionally result in an exciting game." — Vladimir Kramnik[70]
[2022-05-20 04:33] pikachumemes. (Pikachu Memes): prismata probably suffers from similar problems

messages.author: pikachumemes.

In particular, the nontraditional starting positions make it difficult for many amateurs to enjoy the game until more familiar positions are achieved. The same is true for world-class players, as many have confessed to me privately. Finally, it also seems to lack an aesthetic quality found in traditional chess, which makes it less appealing for both players and viewers, even if it does occasionally result in an exciting game." — Vladimir Kramnik[70]

threads.formatted_text:
--- Thread (3 messages, 2022-05-20) ---
[2022-05-20 06:22] pikachumemes. (Pikachu Memes): I think FisherRandom is the more accurate comparison. Consider that a game of Prismata can be effectively decided on T2 (or even T1 with things like wild drone and/or galvani) in (relatively) high level play by making the wrong buys; this reminds me of fisher random where people can think super hard on turn 4(!). Viz: https://en.chessbase.com/post/the-problem-with-chess960
The problem with Chess960
Two weeks ago there was a World Championship — in Chess960, a variant that symmetrically shuffles the position of the pieces behind the row of pawns. The game has gained some popularity since it eliminates the staggering amount of preparation that is required in regular chess. But Chess960 has a few problems that probably prevent it from really ...
[2022-05-20 06:23] pikachumemes. (Pikachu Memes): "Here's another example: Svidler is still wondering, on move five, if he can move a pawn and not lose instantly, while Anand is pondering his 22nd move in a very interesting position. In the commentary booths, the GMs were discussing Anand's options with great excitement – he seemed to be struggling to equalize with white in a Sveshnikov! They were completely silent on the Aronian-Svidler game, as nobody had the faintest idea of what was going on. I think it was Tim Krabbé who compared commenting on a Fischer Random game to conducting a guided tour of an art gallery that you are visiting for the first time. Very apt."
[2022-05-20 06:29] desangre: it definitely is a better comparison gameplay wise. I was implicitly meaning that I think the Fischer random audience isn't that big to be considered "targettable" from a marketing standpoint? And ironically, some of the reasons chess players wouldn't play fischer, are similar to what they would feel about Prismata too I guess.

messages.author: pikachumemes.
messages.content:
I think FisherRandom is the more accurate comparison. Consider that a game of Prismata can be effectively decided on T2 (or even T1 with things like wild drone and/or galvani) in (relatively) high level play by making the wrong buys; this reminds me of fisher random where people can think super hard on turn 4(!). Viz: https://en.chessbase.com/post/the-problem-with-chess960
The problem with Chess960
Two weeks ago there was a World Championship — in Chess960, a variant that symmetrically shuffles the position of the pieces behind the row of pawns. The game has gained some popularity since it eliminates the staggering amount of preparation that is required in regular chess. But Chess960 has a few problems that probably prevent it from really ...

messages.author: pikachumemes.
messages.content: "Here's another example: Svidler is still wondering, on move five, if he can move a pawn and not lose instantly, while Anand is pondering his 22nd move in a very interesting position. In the commentary booths, the GMs were discussing Anand's options with great excitement – he seemed to be struggling to equalize with white in a Sveshnikov! They were completely silent on the Aronian-Svidler game, as nobody had the faintest idea of what was going on. I think it was Tim Krabbé who compared commenting on a Fischer Random game to conducting a guided tour of an art gallery that you are visiting for the first time. Very apt."

[2022-07-16 07:46] .sha6001 (Sha): I want to make a strategy game that's like primata, void of any rng, any tips for that?
[2022-07-16 07:48] sano712 (sano): make sure to have a playerbase greater than 10
[2022-07-16 08:00] amalloy: prismata mainly drew ideas from magic, chess, dominion, and starcraft. two of those have no randomness, so you could just rerelease those
[2022-07-16 08:03] .sha6001 (Sha): I now know why prismata is basically build order simulator
[2022-07-16 16:49] spiritfryer (Spyrfyr): HoMM3 multiplayer has a bidding system to decide who goes first, and to decide who gets first pick of faction
[2022-07-16 16:50] spiritfryer (Spyrfyr): Prismata is not granular enough for a bidding system to fix things prolly, but if it was then it'd prolly be a fairly good lazy but effective fix
[2022-07-16 16:50] spiritfryer (Spyrfyr): Anyway sorry it's #ask_a_dev we can continue in another channel

--- Thread (4 messages, 2022-08-03) ---
[2022-08-03 10:19] velizar_ (Name): a more continuous version of Prismata can be really nice
[2022-08-03 10:19] velizar_ (Name): with discrete values like this, playing the game gets a lot fiddlier
[2022-08-03 10:20] velizar_ (Name): because you need to get things exactly right, and not be off by 1. You can't buy 97% of a Centurion.
[2022-08-03 10:21] velizar_ (Name): which is also the sort of practice which gets rusty if you stop playing the game, I suspect.

messages.author: velizar_
messages.content: a more continuous version of Prismata can be really nice

--- Thread (1 messages, 2022-08-05) ---
[2022-08-05 13:02] .holyfire (Holy-Fire): Looks like there are some interesting discussions here I need to catch up on.

Did this thread ever continue in another channel?

I'll mention that I had some thoughts for a bidding system for Prismata, or more precisely, a cake-cutting system. I don't think granularity is much of a problem - you can have bids of the form "Gain 1 gold on turn X and another 1 gold on turn Y", this leads to reasonably granular possibilities.

messages.author: .holyfire
messages.content:
Looks like there are some interesting discussions here I need to catch up on.

Did this thread ever continue in another channel?

I'll mention that I had some thoughts for a bidding system for Prismata, or more precisely, a cake-cutting system. I don't think granularity is much of a problem - you can have bids of the form "Gain 1 gold on turn X and another 1 gold on turn Y", this leads to reasonably granular possibilities.

[2022-08-05 18:49] keithrice (Keith Rice): I'm curious how ranked matchmaking works in Prismata given the low player pool --- what stops two people from just playing against one another over and over, and one of them intentionally throwing for the sake of boosting the other player? Also does prismata use Elo or another system?
[2022-08-05 18:54] awaclus: nothing in principle, but why would you tank your own rating just to boost someone else

[2022-08-08 18:58] elyot: We would ban somebody if we say them doing this. There are some measures to detect it automatically and flag us, or prevent the match from being made.
[2022-08-08 19:02] keithrice (Keith Rice): Makes sense. Thanks for the reply. Did you ever see it occur?
[2022-08-08 19:05] elyot: I don't think anybody ever really tried super hard to abuse or corrupt our rankings, but we did find some people screwing around a little

messages.author: elyot
messages.content: We would ban somebody if we say them doing this. There are some measures to detect it automatically and flag us, or prevent the match from being made.

messages.author: elyot
messages.content: I don't think anybody ever really tried super hard to abuse or corrupt our rankings, but we did find some people screwing around a little

threads.formatted_text:
--- Thread (8 messages, 2023-03-19 to 2023-03-21) ---
[2023-03-19 20:33] zera777 (Zera): @Elyot
How were the Prismata puzzles designed?
How did you determine the difficulty labels on them?
Why do they go from ⭐ to 💀?
[2023-03-20 22:21] elyot: I made most of them myself. A few were designed by other people but all of them were tweaked by me. They're created in text files, and you can press 1 button to reload the text file and restart the mission, so it's very fast to playtest them. There are even cheats to kill all enemies or give infinite resources, which can be used to skip to certain phases of fights.

I don't actually use spreadsheets to design the mission, it's mostly by feel and on paper, and very rarely (like with quandary of limitations) some spreadsheeting. electrovorology was designed with some AI assistance. You do end up spending a lot of time playtesting to nail the difficulty level
[2023-03-20 22:22] elyot: The most interesting part for me was actually coming up with the ideas and the themes... like trying to take a lot of the most interesting strategic moments in Prismata and turning them into missions where you had to do that one thing (or were encouraged to do it) in order to win
[2023-03-20 22:25] siepu: then am impressed with how tight the puzzles are!
[2023-03-20 22:26] siepu: but then im a noob, just refunded patricks parablox cause too hard lol
[2023-03-21 08:15] shadourow (Rando): refunding this masterpiece of an indie game :ReigeRage:
[2023-03-21 08:16] shadourow (Rando): But yeah, it kinda feels like when I first learned about recursing in code, it's really hard to wrap your head around how it work but when you do, it's dark magic
[2023-03-21 08:16] shadourow (Rando): And you're the archimage

messages.author: zera777
messages.content:
@Elyot
How were the Prismata puzzles designed?
How did you determine the difficulty labels on them?
Why do they go from ⭐ to 💀?

messages.author: elyot
messages.content:
I made most of them myself. A few were designed by other people but all of them were tweaked by me. They're created in text files, and you can press 1 button to reload the text file and restart the mission, so it's very fast to playtest them. There are even cheats to kill all enemies or give infinite resources, which can be used to skip to certain phases of fights.

I don't actually use spreadsheets to design the mission, it's mostly by feel and on paper, and very rarely (like with quandary of limitations) some spreadsheeting. electrovorology was designed with some AI assistance. You do end up spending a lot of time playtesting to nail the difficulty level

messages.author: elyot
messages.content: The most interesting part for me was actually coming up with the ideas and the themes... like trying to take a lot of the most interesting strategic moments in Prismata and turning them into missions where you had to do that one thing (or were encouraged to do it) in order to win

messages.author: siepu
messages.content: then am impressed with how tight the puzzles are!

messages.author: siepu
messages.content: but then im a noob, just refunded patricks parablox cause too hard lol

messages.author: shadourow
messages.content: refunding this masterpiece of an indie game :ReigeRage:

messages.author: shadourow
messages.content: But yeah, it kinda feels like when I first learned about recursing in code, it's really hard to wrap your head around how it work but when you do, it's dark magic

messages.author: shadourow
messages.content: And you're the archimage

--- Thread (2 messages, 2024-02-15) ---
[2024-02-15 20:46] cale: @Elyot lol game crashed taking this screenshot (using Proton to run the game, it always crashes when tabbing out), but what's going on with this pattern grid? I got frustrated and brute forced it using foresight hoping that the actual solution would help me understand what sort of pattern the game wanted me to look for, but even given the solution I still don't see it. There was one other pattern puzzle in this enclave that I got just by messing around (without using foresight) because the solution was symmetric, but it was not obvious to me why that, and not some other colouring was the solution either. It feels weird/wrong to be guessing or bruteforcing with hints. Should I be looking for clues outside the puzzles?
[2024-02-15 20:51] awaclus: ||the pattern in this case is not a visual pattern, but it is contained entirely within the puzzle|| (spoiler doesn't contain the actual answer to what pattern it is, but it contains a hint)

threads.formatted_text:
--- Thread (6 messages, 2024-02-15 to 2024-02-16) ---
[2024-02-15 21:16] cale: Would the solution be the same if the grid were subdivided entirely into single squares, or is gluing tiles together only helping rule out certain rules?
[2024-02-15 21:20] awaclus: ||you could separate each block and the solution would be the same||
[2024-02-15 21:20] cale: okay, cool (I still don't get it, but ||that helps limit the space of things I have to try a lot||)
[2024-02-16 02:05] elyot: No, the merged multi-times are essential
[2024-02-16 02:06] cale: ohh, okay, well, that's a completely different set of things to look at then
[2024-02-16 02:08] cale: Also, any chance of a Linux build of this (even a closed alpha/beta build we could try)? It's just barely playable, but real crashy and pretty sluggish under Proton.

messages.author: cale
messages.content: Would the solution be the same if the grid were subdivided entirely into single squares, or is gluing tiles together only helping rule out certain rules?

messages.author: awaclus
messages.content: ||you could separate each block and the solution would be the same||

messages.author: cale
messages.content: okay, cool (I still don't get it, but ||that helps limit the space of things I have to try a lot||)

messages.author: elyot
messages.content: No, the merged multi-times are essential

messages.author: cale
messages.content: ohh, okay, well, that's a completely different set of things to look at then

messages.author: cale
messages.content: Also, any chance of a Linux build of this (even a closed alpha/beta build we could try)? It's just barely playable, but real crashy and pretty sluggish under Proton.

threads.formatted_text:
--- Thread (2 messages, 2024-04-22) ---
[2024-04-22 14:22] elyot: I don't know about your laptop, but we did optimize the game a lot, especially the last 6 months before release. Not sure what we will do for future Lunarch games, it depends whether we're self-publishing or working with another publisher. I kinda like doing the community management stuff myself, but it was definitely time-saving to have somebody else do it.
[2024-04-22 14:26] elyot: For Jelly Is Sticky, I'd love to work on more content but I need @David's help and he has not been available very much

messages.author: elyot
messages.content: I don't know about your laptop, but we did optimize the game a lot, especially the last 6 months before release. Not sure what we will do for future Lunarch games, it depends whether we're self-publishing or working with another publisher. I kinda like doing the community management stuff myself, but it was definitely time-saving to have somebody else do it.

messages.author: elyot
messages.content: For Jelly Is Sticky, I'd love to work on more content but I need @David's help and he has not been available very much

[2024-04-22 14:37] elyot: I think it's something Behaviour Interactive does for most or all of their games, they let the community run the subreddits and the discords. Not really the way I prefer to do it, but TBH with Islands of Insight the community is doing a great job

messages.content: I think it's something Behaviour Interactive does for most or all of their games, they let the community run the subreddits and the discords. Not really the way I prefer to do it, but TBH with Islands of Insight the community is doing a great job

threads.formatted_text:
--- Thread (3 messages, 2024-05-04 to 2024-05-06) ---
[2024-05-04 10:51] .bananasplit (Bananasplit): @Elyot What's up? What is Lunarch studios up to these days? When do we get prismata 2?! 😏
[2024-05-04 10:58] .bananasplit (Bananasplit): Island of Insights looks very promising
[2024-05-06 02:23] cale: I really hope it hangs in there. The grid puzzles are awesome and make the thing basically a must-buy for anyone who loves solving logic puzzles. I feel like it's operating at a small fraction of its potential as a multiplayer game though. A way for players to make and trade puzzles with each other, and to communicate with each other in-game seems like it would really go a long way.

messages.author: .bananasplit
messages.content: @Elyot What's up? What is Lunarch studios up to these days? When do we get prismata 2?! 😏

messages.author: .bananasplit
messages.content: Island of Insights looks very promising

messages.author: cale
messages.content: I really hope it hangs in there. The grid puzzles are awesome and make the thing basically a must-buy for anyone who loves solving logic puzzles. I feel like it's operating at a small fraction of its potential as a multiplayer game though. A way for players to make and trade puzzles with each other, and to communicate with each other in-game seems like it would really go a long way.
